title: Shingles (Herpes Zoster)
oneLiner: Acute reactivation of varicella-zoster virus causing painful unilateral dermatomal vesicular rash; common in elderly/immunocompromised, risk of post-herpetic neuralgia (PHN) & ocular/neurological emergencies.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Zoster ophthalmicus (Hutchinson sign – vesicle on tip of nose)**: immediate ophthalmology review – can cause corneal perforation, uveitis, glaucoma, vision loss within hours  
- **Ramsay Hunt syndrome (geniculate ganglion)** : unilateral facial palsy + vesicles in ear canal/tongue + hearing loss/loss of taste → urgent ENT/neurology – may lead to permanent facial nerve damage if antivirals not started within 72h  
- **Disseminated zoster (visceral involvement)** : widespread rash (>20 lesions outside 2 adjacent dermatomes) + fever + pneumonitis/encephalitis → IV aciclovir, admit, call ID/microbiologist
sections.Presentation:
- **Prodrome** (2-3 days before rash): localised burning, tingling, shooting pain in a dermatome – often mistaken for myocardial infarction, renal colic, etc.  
- **Rash**: unilateral, dermatomal, red base → clusters of vesicles → pustules → crusts over 7-10 days  
- **Pain**: acute neuritic pain (burning, stabbing, allodynia) – frequently severe  
- **Systemic symptoms**: fever, malaise, headache (especially if large dermatome or immunocompromised)  
- **Key exam findings**:  
  - Rash respects midline – never crosses to opposite side  
  - Hutchinson sign: vesicle on tip of nose indicates nasociliary nerve involvement → high risk of ocular complications  
  - Ear canal/tongue vesicles + facial palsy = Ramsay Hunt  
  - Lymphadenopathy in draining nodal basin
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Herpes simplex (HSV)** – more recurrent, less strictly dermatomal, often perioral/genital; PCR can differentiate  
- **Contact dermatitis** – no prodromal pain, linear pattern from plant exposure, not dermatomal  
- **Erysipelas/cellulitis** – no vesicles, warm shiny skin, sharp border, systemic signs earlier  
- **Impetigo** – honey-coloured crusts, no dermatomal pattern, more superficial  
- ****Splitter:** dermatomal distribution of vesicles + prodromal pain → shingles until proven otherwise; if recurrent and same site → HSV**
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** clinical diagnosis typical; Tzanck smear rarely done (multinucleated giant cells); **urine dip** if abdominal pain to exclude pancreatitis/renal colic mimicking prodrome  
- **Bloods:** FBC (lymphopenia), U&E + creatinine (baseline for aciclovir dosing), LFTs, glucose (diabetes risk factor)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - Not routinely needed  
  - CT head/MRI brain if neurological signs (encephalitis, cranial nerve palsy)  
  - Chest X-ray if disseminated or pneumonia suspected  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Viral PCR from vesicle swab (gold standard – confirm VZV, differentiate from HSV)  
  - Direct immunofluorescence (if PCR unavailable)  
  - **Ophthalmic slit-lamp examination** if any eye symptoms or Hutchinson sign  
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Slit-lamp eye exam** (call ophthalmology) – corneal dendrites/keratitis requires urgent topical antivirals  
  - **HIV test** in young patients (<50) with severe/extensive shingles or recurrent episodes
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Pain control: paracetamol 1g IV/PO + NSAIDs (if no renal impairment) + neuropathic agents (gabapentin or amitriptyline short-term)  
  - Start antivirals if within 72h of rash onset **or** if new vesicles still forming/severe pain – even if >72h in immunocompromised or ophthalmic cases  
  - **If ophthalmic or Ramsay Hunt**: IV aciclovir + immediate specialist referral  
  - Consider IV fluids if dehydrated (elderly, unable to swallow)  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Regular pain review (scale 0-10), escalate neuropathic agents as needed  
  - Monitor for dissemination (check all skin surfaces, respiratory rate, mental status)  
  - Keep rash covered (dry dressings) to prevent transmission to varicella-naive contacts  
  - If immunosuppressed – liaise with microbiology/infectious diseases  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Continue oral antivirals for total 7 days  
  - Neuropathic pain management (gabapentin/pregabalin – titrate slowly; or amitriptyline at night)  
  - Consider antiviral therapy for PHN prevention – no good evidence, but treat pain aggressively  
  - Vaccination after acute episode: Shingrix (recombinant) for adults ≥50 years (2 doses 2-6 months apart)  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - IV fluids: 0.9% NaCl at maintenance rate (e.g., 1L/8h if NBM)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily if reduced mobility  
  - Diet: normal as tolerated unless NBM for IV therapy or sedation  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (increase to 1-hourly if NEWS≥5 or immunocompromised)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (oral):** Aciclovir 800mg PO 5 times daily for 7 days  
- **First-line (IV):** Aciclovir 5-10 mg/kg IV 8-hourly (dose based on renal function) – for ophthalmic, Ramsay Hunt, disseminated, immunocompromised, severe pain / new vesicles after 72h  
- **Alternative:** Valaciclovir 1g PO 3 times daily for 7 days (better bioavailability, less frequent dosing)  
- **Pain:**  
  - Paracetamol 1g PO/IV 4-6 hourly (max 4g/24h)  
  - Gabapentin start 300mg PO nocte, titrate up (elderly start 100mg)  
  - Amitriptyline 10-25mg PO nocte (start low in elderly)  
- **Topical:** Not recommended – no benefit  
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**  
  - NSAIDs avoid if eGFR<30 or older/volume depleted  
  - Aciclovir dose adjustment for eGFR 10-25: 800mg PO every 8h; eGFR<10: 800mg PO every 12h  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** All antivirals – check eGFR before prescribing; aciclovir neurotoxicity (confusion, hallucinations) at high levels  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Aciclovir – forgetting to adjust dose in renal impairment → neurotoxicity (seizures, coma) – always check creatinine**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Zoster ophthalmicus (keratitis, uveitis, glaucoma, vision loss)  
  - Ramsay Hunt syndrome (facial palsy, hearing loss, tinnitus, vertigo)  
  - Disseminated zoster (pneumonitis, encephalitis, hepatitis) – high mortality in immunocompromised  
  - Secondary bacterial superinfection (impetiginisation, cellulitis)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Post-herpetic neuralgia (pain >90 days after rash healing) – most common; risk increases with age >50, severe acute pain, ophthalmic involvement  
  - Scarring, hyper/hypopigmentation
sections.Prognosis:
- Immunocompetent adults: rash heals in 2-4 weeks; PHN occurs in 10-20% (33% in >60 years)  
- Mortality rare unless disseminated or encephalitis; risk increases with age and immunocompromise  
- PHN resolves over months in most, but can be chronic in elderly
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Check eye every case** – look for Hutchinson sign and ask about vision/redness/photophobia; refer ophthalmology even if no eye symptoms if vesicle on nose  
- **Start antivirals early** – even if >72h, if new vesicles or severe pain, they still reduce viral shedding and pain duration  
- **Prevent transmission** – advise patient to keep rash covered, avoid contact with pregnant women (non-immune), neonates, and immunosuppressed until crusted over (typically 5-7 days)
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t prescribe steroids alone** (without antivirals) in acute shingles – may worsen dissemination  
- **Don’t ignore eye symptoms** – even mild redness/tearing can be early signs of serious ocular disease  
- **Don’t use topical antivirals** – not effective  
- **Don’t rely on topical creams for pain** – use systemic neuropathic agents early
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Severe headache, photophobia, confusion, neck stiffness (suspected meningitis/encephalitis)  
  - Vision changes, eye pain, redness, new discharge  
  - Rash spreading widely (>20 lesions or beyond 2 dermatomes)  
  - Increasing pain, purulence (bacterial superinfection), fever >38°C  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP within 1 week for pain review and PHN screening  
  - If ophthalmic: ophthalmology clinic within 48h  
  - Consider vaccination after ≥1 year or after acute episode when rash crusted (Shingrix)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) immediately if:**  
  - Any suspicion of disseminated zoster (widespread rash, fever, organ involvement)  
  - Zoster ophthalmicus – must see ophthalmology same day  
  - Ramsay Hunt syndrome – ENT/neurology same day  
  - Immunocompromised patient (chemotherapy, transplant, HIV, steroid therapy) – likely need IV aciclovir and ID consult  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or signs of meningismus/encephalitis (GCS<15, focal neurology, seizure)  
- **Consider ICU if:**  
  - Respiratory failure from VZV pneumonitis  
  - Encephalitis with decreased consciousness/status epilepticus  
  - Severe sepsis from secondary superinfection

rawMarkdown:
## Shingles (Herpes Zoster)
**One-liner:** Acute reactivation of varicella-zoster virus causing painful unilateral dermatomal vesicular rash; common in elderly/immunocompromised, risk of post-herpetic neuralgia (PHN) & ocular/neurological emergencies.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Zoster ophthalmicus (Hutchinson sign – vesicle on tip of nose)**: immediate ophthalmology review – can cause corneal perforation, uveitis, glaucoma, vision loss within hours  
- **Ramsay Hunt syndrome (geniculate ganglion)** : unilateral facial palsy + vesicles in ear canal/tongue + hearing loss/loss of taste → urgent ENT/neurology – may lead to permanent facial nerve damage if antivirals not started within 72h  
- **Disseminated zoster (visceral involvement)** : widespread rash (>20 lesions outside 2 adjacent dermatomes) + fever + pneumonitis/encephalitis → IV aciclovir, admit, call ID/microbiologist  

### Presentation
- **Prodrome** (2-3 days before rash): localised burning, tingling, shooting pain in a dermatome – often mistaken for myocardial infarction, renal colic, etc.  
- **Rash**: unilateral, dermatomal, red base → clusters of vesicles → pustules → crusts over 7-10 days  
- **Pain**: acute neuritic pain (burning, stabbing, allodynia) – frequently severe  
- **Systemic symptoms**: fever, malaise, headache (especially if large dermatome or immunocompromised)  
- **Key exam findings**:  
  - Rash respects midline – never crosses to opposite side  
  - Hutchinson sign: vesicle on tip of nose indicates nasociliary nerve involvement → high risk of ocular complications  
  - Ear canal/tongue vesicles + facial palsy = Ramsay Hunt  
  - Lymphadenopathy in draining nodal basin  

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Herpes simplex (HSV)** – more recurrent, less strictly dermatomal, often perioral/genital; PCR can differentiate  
- **Contact dermatitis** – no prodromal pain, linear pattern from plant exposure, not dermatomal  
- **Erysipelas/cellulitis** – no vesicles, warm shiny skin, sharp border, systemic signs earlier  
- **Impetigo** – honey-coloured crusts, no dermatomal pattern, more superficial  
- ****Splitter:** dermatomal distribution of vesicles + prodromal pain → shingles until proven otherwise; if recurrent and same site → HSV**

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** clinical diagnosis typical; Tzanck smear rarely done (multinucleated giant cells); **urine dip** if abdominal pain to exclude pancreatitis/renal colic mimicking prodrome  
- **Bloods:** FBC (lymphopenia), U&E + creatinine (baseline for aciclovir dosing), LFTs, glucose (diabetes risk factor)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - Not routinely needed  
  - CT head/MRI brain if neurological signs (encephalitis, cranial nerve palsy)  
  - Chest X-ray if disseminated or pneumonia suspected  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Viral PCR from vesicle swab (gold standard – confirm VZV, differentiate from HSV)  
  - Direct immunofluorescence (if PCR unavailable)  
  - **Ophthalmic slit-lamp examination** if any eye symptoms or Hutchinson sign  
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Slit-lamp eye exam** (call ophthalmology) – corneal dendrites/keratitis requires urgent topical antivirals  
  - **HIV test** in young patients (<50) with severe/extensive shingles or recurrent episodes  

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Pain control: paracetamol 1g IV/PO + NSAIDs (if no renal impairment) + neuropathic agents (gabapentin or amitriptyline short-term)  
  - Start antivirals if within 72h of rash onset **or** if new vesicles still forming/severe pain – even if >72h in immunocompromised or ophthalmic cases  
  - **If ophthalmic or Ramsay Hunt**: IV aciclovir + immediate specialist referral  
  - Consider IV fluids if dehydrated (elderly, unable to swallow)  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Regular pain review (scale 0-10), escalate neuropathic agents as needed  
  - Monitor for dissemination (check all skin surfaces, respiratory rate, mental status)  
  - Keep rash covered (dry dressings) to prevent transmission to varicella-naive contacts  
  - If immunosuppressed – liaise with microbiology/infectious diseases  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Continue oral antivirals for total 7 days  
  - Neuropathic pain management (gabapentin/pregabalin – titrate slowly; or amitriptyline at night)  
  - Consider antiviral therapy for PHN prevention – no good evidence, but treat pain aggressively  
  - Vaccination after acute episode: Shingrix (recombinant) for adults ≥50 years (2 doses 2-6 months apart)  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - IV fluids: 0.9% NaCl at maintenance rate (e.g., 1L/8h if NBM)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily if reduced mobility  
  - Diet: normal as tolerated unless NBM for IV therapy or sedation  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (increase to 1-hourly if NEWS≥5 or immunocompromised)  

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (oral):** Aciclovir 800mg PO 5 times daily for 7 days  
- **First-line (IV):** Aciclovir 5-10 mg/kg IV 8-hourly (dose based on renal function) – for ophthalmic, Ramsay Hunt, disseminated, immunocompromised, severe pain / new vesicles after 72h  
- **Alternative:** Valaciclovir 1g PO 3 times daily for 7 days (better bioavailability, less frequent dosing)  
- **Pain:**  
  - Paracetamol 1g PO/IV 4-6 hourly (max 4g/24h)  
  - Gabapentin start 300mg PO nocte, titrate up (elderly start 100mg)  
  - Amitriptyline 10-25mg PO nocte (start low in elderly)  
- **Topical:** Not recommended – no benefit  
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**  
  - NSAIDs avoid if eGFR<30 or older/volume depleted  
  - Aciclovir dose adjustment for eGFR 10-25: 800mg PO every 8h; eGFR<10: 800mg PO every 12h  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** All antivirals – check eGFR before prescribing; aciclovir neurotoxicity (confusion, hallucinations) at high levels  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Aciclovir – forgetting to adjust dose in renal impairment → neurotoxicity (seizures, coma) – always check creatinine**

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Zoster ophthalmicus (keratitis, uveitis, glaucoma, vision loss)  
  - Ramsay Hunt syndrome (facial palsy, hearing loss, tinnitus, vertigo)  
  - Disseminated zoster (pneumonitis, encephalitis, hepatitis) – high mortality in immunocompromised  
  - Secondary bacterial superinfection (impetiginisation, cellulitis)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Post-herpetic neuralgia (pain >90 days after rash healing) – most common; risk increases with age >50, severe acute pain, ophthalmic involvement  
  - Scarring, hyper/hypopigmentation  

### Prognosis
- Immunocompetent adults: rash heals in 2-4 weeks; PHN occurs in 10-20% (33% in >60 years)  
- Mortality rare unless disseminated or encephalitis; risk increases with age and immunocompromise  
- PHN resolves over months in most, but can be chronic in elderly  

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Check eye every case** – look for Hutchinson sign and ask about vision/redness/photophobia; refer ophthalmology even if no eye symptoms if vesicle on nose  
- **Start antivirals early** – even if >72h, if new vesicles or severe pain, they still reduce viral shedding and pain duration  
- **Prevent transmission** – advise patient to keep rash covered, avoid contact with pregnant women (non-immune), neonates, and immunosuppressed until crusted over (typically 5-7 days)  

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t prescribe steroids alone** (without antivirals) in acute shingles – may worsen dissemination  
- **Don’t ignore eye symptoms** – even mild redness/tearing can be early signs of serious ocular disease  
- **Don’t use topical antivirals** – not effective  
- **Don’t rely on topical creams for pain** – use systemic neuropathic agents early  

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Severe headache, photophobia, confusion, neck stiffness (suspected meningitis/encephalitis)  
  - Vision changes, eye pain, redness, new discharge  
  - Rash spreading widely (>20 lesions or beyond 2 dermatomes)  
  - Increasing pain, purulence (bacterial superinfection), fever >38°C  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP within 1 week for pain review and PHN screening  
  - If ophthalmic: ophthalmology clinic within 48h  
  - Consider vaccination after ≥1 year or after acute episode when rash crusted (Shingrix)  

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) immediately if:**  
  - Any suspicion of disseminated zoster (widespread rash, fever, organ involvement)  
  - Zoster ophthalmicus – must see ophthalmology same day  
  - Ramsay Hunt syndrome – ENT/neurology same day  
  - Immunocompromised patient (chemotherapy, transplant, HIV, steroid therapy) – likely need IV aciclovir and ID consult  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or signs of meningismus/encephalitis (GCS<15, focal neurology, seizure)  
- **Consider ICU if:**  
  - Respiratory failure from VZV pneumonitis  
  - Encephalitis with decreased consciousness/status epilepticus  
  - Severe sepsis from secondary superinfection
